subtopic: Intrapartum CTG - recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability
stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 40+3 in spontaneous labour has had an epidural sited two hours ago with a top-up given 25 minutes ago for a left-sided breakthrough. Her booking BMI was 24, she is a non-smoker, has no medical comorbidities, and the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. The 36-week growth scan showed an appropriately grown fetus on the 55th centile with normal Dopplers. The last vaginal examination 40 minutes ago showed 8 cm dilatation, +0 station, direct OA, intact caput, no moulding. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 6 hours ago with clear liquor. Oxytocin augmentation was started 90 minutes ago for slow progress between 6 and 8 cm and is currently at 12 mU/min with contractions 4 in 10, lasting 70 seconds, palpating moderate. Maternal observations are stable: BP 118/72, HR 88, T 36.9, SpO2 99% on room air. The CTG over the last 30 minutes shows a baseline of 145 bpm, reduced variability of 3 to 5 bpm, no accelerations, and recurrent late decelerations occurring with more than 50% of contractions for the last 25 minutes. There has been no prolonged deceleration. The mother is comfortable and the partogram shows progress consistent with primary dysfunctional labour. The obstetric registrar is at the bedside and theatre is available.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: 8 cm, +0, direct OA, clear liquor. Uterus soft between contractions. Palpates 4 in 10 moderate.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 145 bpm, variability 3-5 bpm, no accelerations, recurrent late decelerations >50% of contractions x 25 min. No tachysystole on tocodynamometer.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?

options.text: Stop oxytocin, reposition to left lateral, give IV crystalloid 500 mL bolus, then perform fetal scalp lactate within 15 minutes
options.rationale: This CTG is FIGO pathological (recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability) but without a sustained bradycardia, so intrauterine resuscitation plus fetal scalp blood sampling for lactate is the appropriate next step before escalating to category 1 caesarean (RANZCOG IFSM 2019; FIGO 2015).

options.text: Continue oxytocin and observe for a further 30 minutes to determine whether the pattern resolves
options.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 25 minutes is pathological; continuing oxytocin worsens fetal acidosis and is contraindicated (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval
options.rationale: Category 1 CS is reserved for acute fetal compromise such as prolonged bradycardia, abruption or cord prolapse; a pathological trace without acute decompensation warrants fetal scalp sampling first when feasible (RANZCOG; NICE NG229).

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously for acute tocolysis and continue oxytocin at the current rate
options.rationale: Terbutaline tocolysis is indicated for tachysystole-related fetal compromise; there is no tachysystole here and continuing oxytocin is inappropriate when the trace is pathological (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform an artificial rupture of membranes and apply a fetal scalp electrode without further intrauterine resuscitation
options.rationale: Membranes are already ruptured and applying an FSE without addressing the pathological pattern is insufficient; intrauterine resuscitation and fetal scalp lactate are the indicated next steps (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: A FIGO pathological CTG without acute decompensation is managed with intrauterine resuscitation (stop oxytocin, left lateral, IV fluid, exclude hypotension, exclude tachysystole, consider tocolysis) and fetal scalp lactate sampling. Lactate >=4.8 mmol/L mandates delivery; 4.2-4.7 repeat in 30 minutes; <4.2 reassuring.
key_points: FIGO pathological = >=2 abnormal features OR sinusoidal OR prolonged bradycardia >5 min
Reduced variability + recurrent late decelerations = pathological
Intrauterine resuscitation mnemonic: SPOILT (Stop oxytocin, Position, Oxygen [no longer routinely recommended], IV fluid, Lower BP causes, Tocolysis)
Fetal scalp lactate cut-offs: <4.2 normal, 4.2-4.7 borderline, >=4.8 abnormal
Category 1 CS target decision-to-delivery 30 minutes
explanation.pearls: Reduced variability is the most predictive single feature of fetal acidosis. Recurrent late decelerations with a flat trace is the pattern that should provoke action even without bradycardia.

sources.label: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019
FIGO consensus guidelines on intrapartum fetal monitoring 2015

subtopic: Prolonged deceleration after epidural top-up - vasopressor choice
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 39+1 in spontaneous labour at 6 cm has an epidural in situ that was sited 90 minutes ago and has provided good analgesia until the last hour, when she began to feel a sharp left-sided sensation with contractions. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no cardiac, respiratory or neurological comorbidities, no allergies, no anaphylaxis history, and her booking bloods including FBC and group and screen were unremarkable. Five minutes ago the anaesthetic registrar gave a top-up of 10 mL of 0.1% ropivacaine with 2 microg/mL fentanyl for breakthrough pain. She is currently positioned semi-recumbent with a left lateral wedge in place. Her BP has now dropped from 122/74 to 84/46 mmHg with a maternal HR of 102. She feels light-headed but is conscious and is not nauseated, has no perioral numbness, no tinnitus and no metallic taste. The CTG shows a prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm that has now been ongoing for 4 minutes after previously reassuring features (baseline 140, variability 10-15, accelerations present). There has been no vaginal bleeding, the uterus is soft between contractions (no hypertonus), the liquor was clear at ARM 30 minutes earlier, and there is no concern for cord prolapse on bedside vaginal examination. The anaesthetic registrar is at the bedside and IV access is patent through a 16G cannula.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/46 mmHg, HR 102 bpm (maternal), T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Awake, light-headed. Uterus soft between contractions. No PV bleeding. Cord not palpable.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm for 4 minutes following epidural top-up.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Ephedrine 6 mg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Ephedrine is acceptable but ANZCA and the contemporary obstetric anaesthesia literature favour phenylephrine for spinal/epidural hypotension because ephedrine crosses the placenta and increases fetal acidaemia (ANZCA; Lee 2018 meta-analysis).

options.text: Metaraminol 0.5 mg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Metaraminol is a reasonable alpha-agonist alternative and is commonly used in Australia, but phenylephrine has the strongest evidence base and is the first-choice vasopressor in obstetric neuraxial hypotension (ANZCA; SOAP consensus 2018).

options.text: Phenylephrine 100 microg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Phenylephrine 50-100 microg IV bolus is the first-line vasopressor for neuraxial-induced maternal hypotension because it preserves uterine blood flow and reduces fetal acidaemia compared with ephedrine; combined with left uterine displacement and a fluid bolus this typically resolves the prolonged deceleration (ANZCA; SOAP consensus 2018).

options.text: Adrenaline 10 microg IV bolus and prepare for category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: Adrenaline boluses are reserved for cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis; this is a treatable neuraxial hypotension and the deceleration should resolve with vasopressor plus position change and fluid (ANZCA).

options.text: Atropine 600 microg IV bolus to correct the maternal heart rate
options.rationale: Maternal heart rate is already 102; atropine treats bradycardia, not hypotension, and would not correct uteroplacental hypoperfusion (ANZCA).

explanation.summary: Prolonged deceleration following an epidural top-up is most commonly due to maternal hypotension reducing uteroplacental perfusion. Manage with full left lateral position, IV crystalloid bolus 250-500 mL, and phenylephrine 50-100 microg IV bolus (first-line). If the deceleration does not resolve within 9 minutes, prepare for category 1 caesarean.
key_points: Phenylephrine first-line for neuraxial hypotension in obstetrics
Ephedrine causes more fetal acidaemia (crosses placenta, beta-agonist on fetal heart)
Metaraminol is an acceptable Australian alternative
Always exclude cord prolapse, abruption and uterine rupture as causes of prolonged deceleration
9-minute rule: prolonged deceleration not recovering requires immediate delivery decision
explanation.pearls: Treat the maternal BP first; the fetal heart rate almost always follows. If you are reaching for atropine for the baby, you have the wrong drug.

sources.label: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia
SOAP Consensus Statement on hypotension management 2018

subtopic: Failure to progress in active first stage - oxytocin augmentation
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour was admitted in established labour 6 hours ago at 5 cm dilatation. She has now been re-examined and is 6 cm, +0 station, direct OP position, with mild caput and no moulding. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 4 hours ago with clear liquor. Contractions are palpating 3 in 10, lasting 40 seconds, of mild intensity. She has been mobile and using nitrous oxide. She is otherwise well, BMI at booking 26, no obstetric or medical comorbidities, GBS swab negative at 36 weeks. The CTG is currently a Dawes-Redman normal pattern with baseline 135, variability 10-15, accelerations present, no decelerations. She is asking for stronger pain relief and what can be done to help labour progress, given that her cervix has only opened 1 cm in 6 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: 6 cm, +0, direct OP, mild caput, no moulding. Contractions 3 in 10, 40 s, mild.
data_table.Investigations: CTG normal. GBS negative. Hb 124 g/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 1-2 mU/min, titrated every 30 minutes to achieve 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes
options.rationale: She meets criteria for primary dysfunctional labour in active phase (<1 cm/h, inadequate contractions, intact CTG, membranes already ruptured). Oxytocin augmentation starting at 1-2 mU/min and doubling every 30 minutes is the correct next step (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Perform an artificial rupture of membranes and reassess in 4 hours before considering oxytocin
options.rationale: Membranes have already ruptured spontaneously; ARM is not available. The next step is oxytocin augmentation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section for failure to progress
options.rationale: Failure to progress is not diagnosed at 6 cm with mild contractions; oxytocin augmentation must be trialled adequately (>=4 hours of effective contractions) before diagnosing labour dystocia warranting CS (RANZCOG; NICE).

options.text: Site an epidural and re-examine in 6 hours without commencing augmentation
options.rationale: Epidural is reasonable for analgesia but waiting 6 hours without augmenting inadequate contractions delays diagnosis of labour dystocia and prolongs labour unnecessarily (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 6 mU/min with no titration interval shorter than 60 minutes
options.rationale: Oxytocin in Australian protocols typically starts at 1-2 mU/min with titration every 30 minutes; starting at 6 mU/min risks tachysystole (KEMH; Royal Women's Vic).

explanation.summary: Primary dysfunctional labour in a nullipara at 6 cm with inadequate contractions and intact membranes is managed with oxytocin augmentation (after ARM if membranes intact). Start at 1-2 mU/min and double every 30 minutes to a usual maximum of 32 mU/min, titrated to 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes. Diagnose labour dystocia only after at least 4 hours of adequate contractions with no progress, then proceed to CS.
key_points: Active phase: >=6 cm with regular contractions (Zhang/ACOG)
Adequate progress nullipara: ~0.5-1 cm/h after 6 cm
Oxytocin start 1-2 mU/min, double every 30 min, max ~32 mU/min
Target: 4-5 contractions per 10 min, 45-60 s, moderate-strong
Diagnose dystocia after >=4 h adequate contractions without progress
explanation.pearls: Direct OP at 6 cm is a common cause of slow progress; many will rotate spontaneously with adequate contractions plus mobility/epidural.

sources.label: KEMH Clinical Guideline: Augmentation of labour
Royal Women's Hospital - Oxytocin in labour

subtopic: Shoulder dystocia - third-line manoeuvre after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure
stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 40+5 with a previous unassisted vaginal birth (3.6 kg, no shoulder dystocia documented) is now in second stage. She has gestational diabetes diagnosed at 26 weeks and managed on metformin 1 g twice daily with a last HbA1c of 5.6% and recent fasting BGLs in target range. Her booking BMI was 31. The estimated fetal weight at the 38-week growth scan was 4.1 kg with a normal amniotic fluid index. After 35 minutes of active pushing the head delivers with a turtle sign immediately recognised on the perineum. The midwife immediately calls for help, places the mother in McRoberts position with her hips hyperflexed and applies suprapubic pressure from the side of the fetal back for two consecutive contractions. The anterior shoulder remains impacted behind the symphysis pubis. The obstetric registrar arrives within one minute and the multidisciplinary team is now organised at the bedside, including neonatal staff. The mother has an epidural in situ with adequate block, the bladder is empty after recent in/out catheter, no episiotomy has been performed yet, and there is no PV bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 96 bpm, BP 124/76, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Head delivered, turtle sign present. Anterior shoulder impacted. Epidural in situ. Bladder empty.
data_table.Investigations: EFW at 38 weeks 4.1 kg. GDM on metformin, HbA1c 5.6%.

lead_in: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure have failed, what is the most appropriate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Perform Zavanelli manoeuvre with cephalic replacement and proceed to immediate caesarean section
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a last-resort manoeuvre reserved for failed internal manoeuvres and delivery of the posterior arm; it is not the next step after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure (RANZCOG; ALSO).

options.text: Perform internal rotation (Rubin II or Woods screw) or delivery of the posterior arm, with episiotomy if access is inadequate
options.rationale: After McRoberts and suprapubic pressure, internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw or reverse Woods) or delivery of the posterior arm are the recommended next steps. Episiotomy is performed only if it improves vaginal access for the operator's hand (RANZCOG; ALSO; PROMPT).

options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure during the next contraction to dislodge the anterior shoulder
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia; it worsens impaction and increases the risk of uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury (RANZCOG).

options.text: Deliberately fracture the fetal clavicle by direct outward pressure on the mid-clavicle
options.rationale: Deliberate cleidotomy is a salvage manoeuvre reserved for cases where all other techniques have failed; it is not the next step (RANZCOG; ALSO).

options.text: Cut a generous mediolateral episiotomy alone and ask the mother to push harder with the next contraction
options.rationale: Episiotomy alone does not relieve a bony dystocia; it only creates space for internal manoeuvres. Maternal pushing alone is ineffective once dystocia is diagnosed (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: RANZCOG/ALSO/PROMPT algorithm for shoulder dystocia: call for help, McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, then internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw, reverse Woods) or delivery of the posterior arm. If these fail consider all-fours (Gaskin), then last-resort options (Zavanelli, symphysiotomy, deliberate cleidotomy). Episiotomy only if it aids access for internal manoeuvres.
key_points: HELPERR mnemonic: Help, Evaluate for Episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter manoeuvres, Remove posterior arm, Roll (all-fours)
Fundal pressure is contraindicated
Document the time of head delivery, manoeuvres used, and head-to-body delivery interval
Brachial plexus injury occurs in 4-16% of shoulder dystocias; permanent in <1%
Risk factors: macrosomia, GDM, prior shoulder dystocia, prolonged second stage
explanation.pearls: If the head delivers with a turtle sign, do not wait to see if a contraction will dislodge the shoulder. Diagnose dystocia, call for help and start the algorithm.

sources.label: ALSO (Advanced Life Support in Obstetrics) Course Manual
PROMPT (Practical Obstetric Multi-Professional Training) Course Manual

subtopic: Cord prolapse - immediate management at full dilatation
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour at 10 cm dilatation has just had a spontaneous rupture of membranes with a large gush of clear liquor. The midwife performing a vaginal examination immediately feels a pulsating loop of cord in the vagina at +0 station. The fetal head is at -2 station and direct OA, with 3/5 of the head still palpable abdominally. The CTG was previously reassuring (baseline 140, variability 10, accelerations present, no decelerations) but now shows a prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm starting within the last minute. The mother is comfortable on her right side with an epidural in situ; her observations are BP 122/78, HR 88, T 36.9, SpO2 99%. She is otherwise well, has no obstetric or medical comorbidities, and her 36-week growth scan was unremarkable. The emergency buzzer has been pulled and the obstetric, anaesthetic and neonatal teams are en route. She has had no previous abdominal surgery, no caesarean section, and is otherwise low risk for surgical complications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: 10 cm, -2 station, direct OA, pulsating cord at +0 in vagina. Liquor clear. Bladder catheterised.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm, starting within the last minute.

lead_in: Which immediate set of actions is most appropriate?

options.text: Reduce the cord back into the uterus through the cervix and proceed to instrumental vaginal delivery in the room
options.rationale: Attempted cord replacement is no longer recommended in current RANZCOG/RCOG guidance because of cord trauma and vasospasm; manual elevation of the presenting part is preferred (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 50).

options.text: Continue oxytocin augmentation, reposition to left lateral and reassess CTG in 5 minutes
options.rationale: Oxytocin must be stopped immediately to reduce contractions compressing the cord; observing for 5 minutes is unsafe in cord prolapse with a prolonged deceleration (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously and proceed immediately to assisted vaginal birth with ventouse
options.rationale: Tocolysis with terbutaline may be considered if there is uterine hyperstimulation or delay in transfer, but ventouse at -2 station is contraindicated (station must be at least +2 for safe ventouse) (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord, place mother in knee-chest or exaggerated Sims, fill the bladder with 500 mL saline if delay anticipated, and arrange category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: At -2 station vaginal delivery is not imminent. Manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord (or bladder filling with 500-700 mL warmed saline if transfer delay) plus knee-chest/Sims position plus category 1 CS within 30 minutes is the standard pathway (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 50).

options.text: Wait for the next contraction at full dilatation and perform a fundal pressure delivery to expedite vaginal birth
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated; the head is at -2 station and vaginal delivery is not feasible. Category 1 CS is required (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse with a non-imminent vaginal delivery requires manual elevation of the presenting part (or bladder filling with 500-700 mL warmed saline for transfer delay), knee-chest or exaggerated Sims position, stop oxytocin, consider tocolysis if hyperstimulation, and category 1 caesarean section with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval.
key_points: Diagnosis: pulsating cord palpated below the presenting part
Manual elevation preferred over cord replacement
Bladder filling 500-700 mL saline if transfer delay
Stop oxytocin; consider terbutaline 250 microg SC if hyperstimulation
Category 1 CS, decision-to-delivery target 30 min (RANZCOG/NICE)
Instrumental vaginal birth only if fully dilated AND station >=+2 AND operator confident
explanation.pearls: Keep the examiner's hand in the vagina elevating the presenting part during transfer; do not handle the cord more than necessary because handling causes vasospasm.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical cord prolapse

subtopic: Instrumental birth - forceps vs ventouse selection
stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 40+0 with an epidural is in active second stage. She has been pushing for 110 minutes without descent of the presenting part beyond +1 station. The fetal head is at +1, direct OA, with no caput or moulding palpable, and 0/5 of the head is palpable abdominally on Crichton assessment. The maternal pelvis was clinically adequate on assessment by both registrar and consultant. The CTG shows a baseline of 145 with variability 5-10 bpm, no accelerations and variable decelerations with most contractions but no late or prolonged decelerations and no sinusoidal pattern. Membranes are ruptured with clear liquor of normal volume. Contractions are 4 in 10 with oxytocin running at 16 mU/min. The mother is exhausted, asking for help to deliver, and consented antenatally to instrumental birth in the room with the obstetric registrar present. The bladder is empty after recent catheter in/out. Adequate epidural top-up has been given and the block is dense to ice. The neonatal team has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 37.0 degC.
data_table.Examination: +1 station, direct OA, 0/5 head palpable abdominally, no caput, no moulding. Bladder empty. Epidural adequate.
data_table.Investigations: CTG suspicious (variable decelerations, reduced variability), not pathological.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate instrument and approach for this delivery?

options.text: Kielland's rotational forceps for a deep transverse arrest
options.rationale: Position is direct OA, not transverse arrest; Kielland's is not indicated. Kielland's is also restricted to experienced operators and is generally reserved for rotational deliveries (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG 26).

options.text: Low-cavity non-rotational outlet forceps (e.g. Neville-Barnes or Simpson) under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Direct OA at +1 station with maternal exhaustion and adequate epidural is suitable for low-cavity non-rotational forceps. Forceps have a lower failure rate than ventouse in maternal exhaustion and a suspicious (but not pathological) CTG (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG 26).

options.text: Sequential ventouse then forceps if ventouse fails
options.rationale: Sequential instrument use significantly increases neonatal morbidity (intracranial haemorrhage, subgaleal haematoma) and is generally avoided unless the head is on the perineum; choose the most likely successful instrument first (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Kiwi OmniCup soft ventouse with traction during contractions only
options.rationale: Ventouse has a higher failure rate than forceps with maternal exhaustion and a suspicious CTG, and a higher rate of cephalohaematoma and subgaleal haemorrhage; forceps are preferred in this scenario (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to category 2 caesarean section
options.rationale: At +1 station with 0/5 palpable abdominally and direct OA, a trial of instrumental birth is appropriate (RANZCOG; NICE).

explanation.summary: For mid-cavity or low-cavity OA position with maternal exhaustion and a suspicious (not pathological) CTG, non-rotational forceps have a higher success rate than ventouse and a lower neonatal cephalohaematoma rate, but a higher rate of maternal perineal trauma. Sequential instrument use is associated with higher neonatal morbidity and should be avoided. Document fetal station, position, caput/moulding, bladder, epidural, and consent prior to commencing.
key_points: Pre-instrumental checklist: full dilatation, ruptured membranes, head <=1/5 palpable abdominally, defined station, defined position, adequate analgesia, empty bladder, consent
Forceps: higher success rate, more maternal trauma
Ventouse: higher failure rate, more cephalohaematoma/subgaleal haemorrhage
Sequential use significantly increases neonatal morbidity
Failed instrumental: stop after 3 contractions with no descent or after 20 minutes total
explanation.pearls: Choose the instrument most likely to succeed first. If you would normally choose ventouse but conditions are marginal (exhaustion, suspicious CTG, OP), forceps usually has the better outcome.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted Vaginal Birth

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 39+4 is in active labour at 7 cm with an epidural. She has a previous lower-segment caesarean section for breech and is having a trial of labour after caesarean. The CTG shows a sudden prolonged deceleration to 60 bpm that has not recovered after 4 minutes despite stopping oxytocin, repositioning her in the left lateral, giving a 500 mL crystalloid bolus and excluding maternal hypotension. The registrar has examined her and there is fresh PV bleeding, scar tenderness and loss of station with the previously palpable head now ballotable abdominally. A decision is made for an immediate category 1 caesarean section for suspected uterine rupture. The anaesthetic and theatre teams are activated by emergency pager and obstetric consultant is informed.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Scar tender, fresh PV bleeding, head ballotable abdominally.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration 60 bpm, not recovering at 4 minutes.

lead_in: What is the recommended decision-to-delivery interval for a category 1 caesarean section?

options.text: 15 minutes
options.rationale: 15 minutes is not the audit standard for category 1 CS in Australian or international guidance; the agreed target is 30 minutes (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Categorisation of urgency for CS

options.text: 60 minutes
options.rationale: 60 minutes is closer to the audit interval for category 2 caesarean section (no immediate threat but expedited delivery required) (NICE NG192).

options.text: 45 minutes
options.rationale: 45 minutes is not the audit standard; the agreed target is 30 minutes for category 1 (NICE; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Categorisation of urgency for CS

options.text: 30 minutes
options.rationale: RANZCOG and NICE agree on a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval for category 1 (immediate threat to maternal or fetal life) caesarean section as the audit standard. Times >30 minutes should be reviewed, but evidence does not support that 30 minutes is a strict outcome threshold (RANZCOG; NICE NG192).
source_refs: RANZCOG Categorisation of urgency for CS

options.text: 75 minutes
options.rationale: 75 minutes exceeds even the category 2 target and is not consistent with any current audit standard (NICE).

explanation.summary: Categorisation of urgency for caesarean section (RANZCOG/NICE): Cat 1 - immediate threat (target 30 min decision-to-delivery); Cat 2 - maternal or fetal compromise, not immediately life-threatening (target 60-75 min); Cat 3 - early delivery needed but no compromise; Cat 4 - delivery timed to suit woman or staff.
key_points: Cat 1: 30 minutes (audit standard)
Cat 2: 60-75 minutes (audit standard)
30 minutes is an audit standard, not a strict outcome threshold
Common Cat 1 indications: cord prolapse, abruption, uterine rupture, scalp pH <7.20, prolonged bradycardia
explanation.pearls: Document time of decision, time of arrival in theatre, time of knife-to-skin, and time of delivery for every Cat 1 CS for departmental audit.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Categorisation of urgency for caesarean section

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 presents at 32+1 weeks with a 30-minute history of moderate fresh vaginal bleeding. She had spontaneous rupture of membranes 20 minutes before the bleeding started, with a sudden gush of clear liquor followed almost immediately by bright red blood. She has not had abdominal pain at any point and reports no abdominal trauma in the preceding week. Her two previous births were both lower-segment caesarean sections (the most recent 18 months ago and the first 4 years ago), both for failure to progress in the first stage; she has not been counselled about VBAC this pregnancy and the antenatal plan was for an elective repeat CS at 39 weeks. Her 20-week morphology scan showed a low-lying anterior placenta (3 cm from the os) and a velamentous cord insertion with a vessel coursing close to the internal os, but a repeat 32-week transvaginal scan to characterise the cord vessel and re-measure placental edge was scheduled for next week. She is otherwise well, BMI 28, non-smoker, no cocaine or amphetamine use, no antihypertensive use, and no history of chronic hypertension or pre-eclampsia in previous pregnancies. On arrival she is mildly tachycardic at 102 with a normal BP of 124/76. The CTG shows a sinusoidal pattern with a baseline of 145 bpm and reduced variability. The uterus is soft and non-tender between contractions and there is no maternal pain on speculum examination.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft, non-tender. Speculum: moderate fresh blood at os, cervix appears closed. SROM confirmed.
data_table.Investigations: 20-week USS: low-lying anterior placenta, velamentous cord, vessel near internal os. Two previous LSCS. CTG: sinusoidal, baseline 145 bpm, reduced variability.

lead_in: What is the most likely cause of the antepartum haemorrhage in this woman?

options.text: Placenta praevia with marginal sinus bleeding from the lower segment
options.rationale: Praevia is suggested by the low-lying placenta but classically causes painless bleeding without ROM as the trigger; the sinusoidal CTG immediately after SROM points strongly to vasa praevia rupture (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG 27a).

options.text: Placental abruption secondary to undisclosed cocaine use or trauma
options.rationale: Abruption typically causes painful bleeding with a tender, irritable or tonic uterus; the uterus here is soft and non-tender, and there is no history of trauma or stimulant use (RANZCOG).

options.text: Vasa praevia with ruptured fetal vessel at the internal os
options.rationale: Velamentous cord insertion with a vessel near the internal os, bleeding triggered immediately by SROM, and a sinusoidal CTG (indicating fetal anaemia) is the classic triad of vasa praevia rupture with fetal exsanguination. Requires immediate category 1 CS (RCOG GTG 27a; RANZCOG).

options.text: Uterine rupture along the previous lower-segment scar
options.rationale: Uterine rupture causes severe maternal pain, scar tenderness, maternal haemodynamic compromise, and often loss of fetal station; the uterus here is soft and non-tender and she is not in labour (RANZCOG VBAC).

options.text: Heavy bloody show from early preterm labour
options.rationale: Show is typically blood-stained mucous in small volume; moderate fresh bleeding after SROM with a sinusoidal trace is not consistent (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia rupture is rare but devastating: bleeding is fetal blood, not maternal, so the fetus exsanguinates rapidly while the mother appears haemodynamically intact. Classic triad: ROM, vaginal bleeding, fetal compromise (often sinusoidal CTG). Requires immediate category 1 CS. Antenatal detection at 20-week morphology scan is the key prevention strategy.
key_points: Vasa praevia: ROM-triggered fetal bleeding, sinusoidal CTG, mother haemodynamically stable
Praevia: painless maternal bleeding, soft non-tender uterus, recurrent
Abruption: painful bleeding (or concealed), tender/tonic uterus, may have DIC
Rupture: severe pain, scar tenderness, haemodynamic collapse, loss of station
Vasa praevia detected antenatally requires elective CS at 34-36 weeks after steroids
explanation.pearls: If the mother looks well but the baby looks terrible (sinusoidal CTG) after ROM, think vasa praevia and get to theatre immediately.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

subtopic: Postpartum haemorrhage - second-line uterotonic after oxytocin
stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth at 40+2 of a 4.0 kg neonate after a 9-hour first stage and a 45-minute second stage with oxytocin augmentation for slow progress in the first stage. Active management of the third stage with IM oxytocin 10 units has been given and the placenta is delivered intact 8 minutes after the baby. There is now ongoing brisk PV bleeding estimated at 1200 mL over 10 minutes with a soft, boggy uterus that is poorly contracted at the umbilicus. The bladder has been catheterised and is empty. Bimanual compression has been commenced. IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 250 mL/h is already running through a 16G cannula. Her observations are BP 96/58, HR 118, T 36.7. She has a history of asthma (mild, intermittent, no recent exacerbations) but no hypertensive disorder of pregnancy and no other medical comorbidities. Blood has been sent for FBC, coagulation, fibrinogen and group and crossmatch.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft and boggy, fundus at umbilicus. Bladder empty. No genital tract trauma visible. Placenta delivered intact.
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 1200 mL. FBC/coags/fibrinogen/X-match sent. Asthma history (mild, intermittent).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step in addition to ongoing IV oxytocin infusion?

options.text: PR misoprostol 800 microg with antiemetic precaution and continue IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Misoprostol is third-line in Australian guidance because of slower onset and higher rates of pyrexia/shivering; ergometrine and carboprost are preferred when available and not contraindicated (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IM carboprost 250 microg, repeat every 15 minutes to a maximum of 8 doses
options.rationale: Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma; ergometrine is the preferred second-line agent in this patient (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IM ergometrine 250 microg (no hypertension contraindication) plus IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Atonic PPH with no contraindication to ergometrine (no hypertension/pre-eclampsia/cardiac disease): ergometrine 250 microg IM (or IV slowly) is second-line, plus IV TXA 1 g over 10 min within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial). Asthma is a relative contraindication to carboprost, not ergometrine (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

options.text: Increase IV oxytocin infusion to 60 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 500 mL/h and observe response for 15 minutes
options.rationale: Maximum oxytocin dose is 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's (or equivalent) over 4 hours per Australian protocols; doses above this risk water intoxication and hyponatraemia, and oxytocin failure should prompt a second-line agent (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Insert an intrauterine balloon tamponade with 500 mL warmed saline immediately and withhold further uterotonics
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is appropriate after failure of medical management; uterotonics (and TXA) should be optimised first (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Atonic PPH stepwise: massage and compression, oxytocin (10 IU IM/IV bolus then 40 IU/1 L IV infusion), ergometrine 250 microg IM/IV (if no contraindication), carboprost 250 microg IM q15min up to 8 doses (avoid in asthma), misoprostol 800 microg PR if other agents unavailable. Give IV TXA 1 g over 10 min within 3 hours of birth. Escalate to balloon tamponade, B-Lynch, internal iliac ligation, hysterectomy.
key_points: Ergometrine contraindicated: hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease, Raynaud's
Carboprost contraindicated: asthma (relative)
Misoprostol side effects: pyrexia, shivering
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial 2017)
Max IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's over 4 hours
explanation.pearls: If hypertensive: avoid ergometrine, use carboprost (if no asthma). If asthma: avoid carboprost, use ergometrine (if no hypertension). If both: misoprostol.

subtopic: Severe pre-eclampsia at 28 weeks - antihypertensive selection
stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 is admitted with a 24-hour history of severe frontal headache, blurred vision and right upper quadrant pain. She booked at 14 weeks with a BP of 116/72 and no proteinuria; her booking BMI was 23 and she has no chronic hypertension, no diabetes and no autoimmune disease. The 20-week morphology scan was normal and the most recent growth scan at 26 weeks showed an appropriately grown fetus with normal Dopplers. Her BP on arrival is 178/116 mmHg confirmed on repeat with a manual cuff after 5 minutes' rest, with 3+ proteinuria on dipstick and a urine PCR of 380 mg/mmol. She is reflexively brisk at 3+ with no clonus, no focal neurology, and no visual field defect on confrontation. Bloods show platelets 92, ALT 184, urate 0.42, creatinine 88, LDH normal, no haemolysis on film, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 4.2. A magnesium sulphate loading dose of 4 g IV over 20 minutes was given 30 minutes ago and a maintenance infusion of 1 g/h is now running. Despite the magnesium load her headache and blurred vision persist and her BP remains 174/112. The CTG is currently reassuring with a baseline of 138 and normal variability. She is otherwise well, has not received antenatal corticosteroids in this pregnancy, has no asthma, no contraindications to any single antihypertensive agent, and the neonatal team has been notified for likely preterm delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 174/112 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Headache, blurred vision persist. Hyperreflexia 3+, no clonus. RUQ tender.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 92 (x10^9/L), ALT 184 U/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, urine PCR 380 mg/mmol, no haemolysis. CTG reassuring.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate additional antihypertensive intervention?

options.text: IV hydralazine 5 mg bolus, then 5-10 mg every 20 minutes (max 30 mg) titrated to BP <160/110
options.rationale: Hydralazine is an acceptable second- or third-line agent in Australian guidance and is associated with more maternal hypotension and fetal distress than labetalol or nifedipine (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: IV labetalol 20 mg bolus, then 40 mg after 10 minutes, then 80 mg every 10 minutes (max cumulative 220 mg) titrated to BP <160/110
options.rationale: Labetalol IV is first-line for acute severe hypertension in pre-eclampsia per SOMANZ 2023 (Australian guidance), with nifedipine immediate-release as an alternative first-line. Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis and does not lower BP. Delivery is indicated after stabilisation given persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium (SOMANZ).

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release 10 mg, repeated every 30 minutes to a maximum of 3 doses
options.rationale: Oral nifedipine immediate-release is an acceptable first-line alternative to IV labetalol per SOMANZ; either is reasonable. However, immediate-release nifedipine carries a risk of overshoot hypotension and is generally favoured when IV access is limited; in a monitored unit with IV access, labetalol is the more controllable first-line agent (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg capsule bitten and swallowed, repeated every 20 minutes as required to a maximum of three doses
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in pregnancy because of unpredictable absorption and risk of severe overshoot hypotension causing fetal compromise; only the oral immediate-release route is acceptable (SOMANZ).

options.text: Increase the magnesium sulphate maintenance infusion from 1 g/h to 2 g/h to achieve blood pressure control
options.rationale: Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis only and does not lower blood pressure. Increasing the infusion risks magnesium toxicity including loss of patellar reflexes and respiratory depression (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium load requires (1) acute BP control to <160/110 with IV labetalol or oral nifedipine IR (first-line per SOMANZ), with hydralazine second-line, and (2) delivery after stabilisation (antenatal steroids if not given and time permits, but delivery should not be unduly delayed). Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis, not BP control.
key_points: Severe HTN target: <160/110 within 30-60 minutes
SOMANZ first-line acute severe HTN: IV labetalol OR oral nifedipine IR
Hydralazine: second/third-line, more maternal hypotension
Magnesium: seizure prophylaxis only (4 g IV load, 1 g/h maintenance)
Persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium = imminent eclampsia = deliver
Antenatal steroids 11.4 mg betamethasone IM x 2 doses 24 h apart if <34+6 weeks
explanation.pearls: Stabilise mother, then deliver. The cure for pre-eclampsia is delivery, and persistent neurological symptoms after a magnesium load are an indication that delivery cannot wait for the full steroid course.

subtopic: Twin delivery - second twin with non-cephalic lie
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 37+3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of Twin 1 (cephalic, 2.6 kg) with intact membranes of the second sac. She has an epidural in situ. After delivery of Twin 1 her uterus relaxes and bedside ultrasound now shows Twin 2 in a transverse lie with the back inferior and the membranes still intact. The estimated fetal weight of Twin 2 at the 36-week scan was 2.8 kg. The CTG of Twin 2 is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm, variability 10-15, accelerations present, no decelerations. The cervix remains fully dilated. The obstetric consultant is in theatre attached to the room, and the anaesthetist confirms a working epidural with a dense block. There is no PV bleeding and the uterus is currently soft. The team has been briefed and the mother consented antenatally to internal manoeuvres and breech extraction for a non-cephalic second twin.

data_table.Examination: Cervix fully dilated. Twin 2 transverse, back inferior, membranes intact. Uterus relaxed.
data_table.Investigations: USS at bedside: transverse lie, back inferior. CTG reassuring.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of Twin 2 at this point?

options.text: External cephalic version under tocolysis followed by ARM when cephalic and longitudinal
options.rationale: ECV for a second twin has lower success rates and higher failure rates than internal podalic version with breech extraction; with the back inferior and a relaxed uterus, internal podalic version is generally preferred by skilled operators (RANZCOG; ACOG).

options.text: ARM immediately to allow the head to descend by gravity
options.rationale: ARM with a transverse lie is contraindicated because of the high risk of cord prolapse; version must occur first (RANZCOG).

options.text: Immediate caesarean section for the second twin
options.rationale: Caesarean for the second twin after vaginal birth of the first is associated with higher maternal morbidity than internal podalic version with breech extraction in experienced hands and is not the first-line approach for a non-cephalic Twin 2 in current Australian practice (RANZCOG; Twin Birth Study 2013).

options.text: Wait 60 minutes for spontaneous version before any intervention
options.rationale: Delay risks cord prolapse on subsequent ROM, abruption from uterine retraction, and a closing cervix; current guidance recommends prompt management of Twin 2 within 30 minutes of Twin 1 (RANZCOG).

options.text: Internal podalic version with breech extraction under continued epidural
options.rationale: Transverse lie of the second twin with intact membranes, a relaxed uterus and a working epidural is the classic indication for internal podalic version with breech extraction by a skilled operator. This avoids caesarean for the second twin and outcomes are equivalent or better when performed by experienced operators (RANZCOG; ACOG).

explanation.summary: For a non-cephalic second twin after delivery of Twin 1, internal podalic version with breech extraction is the preferred approach by a skilled operator with a working epidural and a relaxed uterus. ECV is an alternative but has higher failure rates. Caesarean for the second twin is reserved for cord prolapse, fetal compromise unresponsive to manoeuvres, or operator inexperience.
key_points: Twin Birth Study (NEJM 2013): planned vaginal birth non-inferior to planned CS at >=32 weeks if Twin 1 cephalic
IPV preferred with intact membranes, relaxed uterus, working epidural
ECV alternative; higher failure rate
Always have theatre ready and the consultant available
Deliver Twin 2 within 30 minutes of Twin 1 to reduce abruption and cord prolapse risk
explanation.pearls: Do not break the membranes of Twin 2 until you have a cephalic or breech longitudinal lie. ROM with a transverse lie is a category 1 emergency.

sources.label: Twin Birth Study (Barrett et al. NEJM 2013)

subtopic: Maternal collapse in labour ward - amniotic fluid embolism
stem: A 36-year-old G4P3 at 39+4 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth at 09:42 after a 4-hour labour augmented with oxytocin from 5 cm for slow progress, ending with a second stage of 30 minutes and an intact perineum. Her three previous births were unassisted vaginal deliveries at term with no complications. Her booking BMI was 26, she has no cardiac, respiratory or thromboembolic history, no allergies, and was on no regular medications. Her booking bloods including FBC, group and screen, and antibody screen were unremarkable, and her 36-week growth scan showed a normally grown fetus. The placenta was delivered by controlled cord traction at 09:48 and active third-stage management with IM oxytocin 10 units had been given immediately after the birth. Two minutes after delivery of the placenta she suddenly develops dyspnoea, agitation and a sense of impending doom while talking to her partner. Within 60 seconds she becomes cyanotic, loses consciousness and her monitor shows BP 60/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, SpO2 78% on 15 L/min via Hudson mask, with the trace transitioning to a narrow-complex tachycardia. Within 90 seconds she becomes pulseless with a rhythm of PEA on the defibrillator. There is now brisk PV bleeding from the uterus despite a soft contracted fundus on bimanual palpation, and oozing from her peripheral IV site and recent venipuncture. She has no history of cardiac disease, no epidural was sited at any point, no local anaesthetic was used apart from 5 mL of 1% lignocaine for a perineal infiltration after the birth, and there were no transfusions or blood products administered during labour. CPR is commenced with manual left uterine displacement and the obstetric, anaesthetic, ICU and haematology teams are paged through the MET emergency call.

data_table.Vital signs: Pre-arrest: BP 60/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, SpO2 78% on 15 L/min O2. Now pulseless with PEA.
data_table.Examination: Cyanotic, unconscious. Brisk PV bleeding from soft contracted uterus. Oozing from IV site.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods drawn: anticipate prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen, low platelets. CTG no longer relevant.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?

options.text: Amniotic fluid embolism with cardiopulmonary collapse and DIC
options.rationale: Sudden peripartum collapse with hypoxia, hypotension and coagulopathy (PPH plus bleeding from puncture sites) is the classic Clark triad of AFE. PEA arrest within minutes of placental delivery in a multipara fits the AFE phenotype; management is high-quality CPR with left uterine displacement, A-OK protocol (atropine, ondansetron, ketorolac), early massive transfusion and cardiac arrest team activation (RANZCOG; AFE Foundation; Clark 2016).

options.text: Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) from perineal lignocaine infiltration
options.rationale: Only 5 mL of 1% lignocaine (50 mg) was used, far below the toxic threshold (3 mg/kg without adrenaline); LAST typically presents with seizures and arrhythmia, not the AFE phenotype (ANZCA; LAST guideline).

options.text: Eclamptic seizure with secondary cardiac arrest
options.rationale: There is no history of hypertension, proteinuria or seizures preceding collapse, and the presentation is hypoxic collapse with coagulopathy rather than seizure activity (SOMANZ).

options.text: Acute pulmonary embolism with right heart failure
options.rationale: PE is on the differential but does not classically cause the early DIC and oozing from IV sites that this woman has; AFE is more consistent with the full clinical picture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock
options.rationale: ACS is possible but rare in a 36-year-old without risk factors and does not explain the coagulopathy/oozing or the temporal relationship with placental delivery (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare (~2-8 per 100,000 births) but devastating cause of maternal collapse, with peripartum hypoxia, hypotension and DIC as the classic triad. Diagnosis is clinical (Clark criteria); management is high-quality CPR with manual left uterine displacement (LUD), A-OK (atropine 1 mg, ondansetron 8 mg, ketorolac 30 mg IV per the controversial AFE Foundation protocol), early massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets), and consideration of perimortem caesarean by 4 minutes if no ROSC.
key_points: Clark criteria (4 required): cardiovascular collapse OR hypotension+respiratory compromise, DIC, intrapartum or within 30 min of placental delivery, no fever
Manual left uterine displacement throughout CPR (not tilt)
Perimortem CS by 4 minutes (4-minute rule) if no ROSC and >=20 weeks
Massive transfusion protocol; expect early cryoprecipitate for low fibrinogen
Differential of obstetric collapse: AFE, PE, eclampsia, MI, anaphylaxis, LAST, intracranial bleed, hypovolaemia
explanation.pearls: If the mother collapses with the placenta still in the room and starts oozing from her IV site, do not wait for confirmation - call MET and run the AFE algorithm now.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium
Clark SL et al. Proposed diagnostic criteria for AFE 2016

stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 was admitted in spontaneous labour 22 hours ago with intact membranes; she had a SROM 20 hours ago and is now at 7 cm with oxytocin augmentation for slow progress. She has just developed a temperature of 38.4 degC sustained on two readings 30 minutes apart, with a maternal tachycardia of 122 bpm and a fetal tachycardia on CTG of 175 bpm with reduced variability. She reports a mild uterine ache between contractions and the liquor on examination is now offensive and meconium-stained (Grade 2). She has no productive cough, no dysuria, no headache and no diarrhoea. Her GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. She has no penicillin allergy. Her observations are BP 102/64, HR 122, RR 22, T 38.4. The decision is made to commence empirical intrapartum antibiotics for suspected chorioamnionitis after IV access, two sets of peripheral blood cultures, a high vaginal swab and a midstream urine sample are obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 122 bpm, RR 22 /min, T 38.4 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Uterus mildly tender between contractions. Offensive Grade 2 meconium-stained liquor. 7 cm dilatation.
data_table.Investigations: GBS negative at 36 weeks. Cultures + HVS + MSU sent. No penicillin allergy.

lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen is most appropriate per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines?

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly alone until delivery
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin alone is the GBS intrapartum prophylaxis regimen and does not cover Gram-negative or anaerobic organisms implicated in chorioamnionitis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam is reserved for treatment failure or severe sepsis with broader Gram-negative concerns; it is not first-line empirical for chorioamnionitis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily plus IV metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone plus metronidazole is reasonable in penicillin allergy but is not the Australian first-line regimen for chorioamnionitis in a non-allergic patient (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines first-line empirical regimen for chorioamnionitis is IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily; metronidazole is added at caesarean section for anaerobic cover (Therapeutic Guidelines; KEMH).

options.text: IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy (immediate hypersensitivity) and adds little against the typical chorioamnionitis pathogen mix; not first-line in a non-allergic patient (Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Chorioamnionitis empirical therapy per Australian Therapeutic Guidelines: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily. Add IV metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly if caesarean section is required. Penicillin allergy (non-immediate): IV cefazolin plus gentamicin. Immediate hypersensitivity: IV clindamycin plus gentamicin. Take blood cultures, HVS and MSU before antibiotics if possible without delaying treatment.
key_points: Diagnostic criteria (Gibbs): maternal fever PLUS >=2 of: maternal tachycardia, fetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, offensive liquor, maternal leucocytosis
First-line: IV ampicillin + gentamicin; add metronidazole if CS
Penicillin allergy (non-severe): cefazolin + gentamicin
Penicillin allergy (immediate): clindamycin + gentamicin
Expedite delivery once antibiotics commenced; do not delay for prolonged labour
Notify paediatrics; neonate will need early-onset sepsis workup
explanation.pearls: Take cultures before antibiotics if you can, but do not delay antibiotics by more than the time it takes to draw blood and put up the giving set.

subtopic: Failed induction - unfavourable cervix after prostaglandins
stem: A 33-year-old G1P0 at 41+5 is undergoing induction of labour for prolonged pregnancy. She has received two doses of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel, 6 hours apart, with the second dose administered 8 hours ago. Her cervix on the most recent assessment is 1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, with the head at -3 station - a Bishop score of 3. She has had no regular contractions and the CTG remains reassuring. Her membranes are intact. She has no contraindication to mechanical methods, no GBS bacteriuria, no previous caesarean section, no antepartum haemorrhage and no fetal compromise. She has no allergies. Her BMI at booking was 24, the morphology scan was normal, and the EFW at 40 weeks was 3.4 kg. The local protocol permits a maximum of two doses of dinoprostone gel in 24 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Bishop score 3 (1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, -3 station). Membranes intact.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Perform an ARM now and commence IV oxytocin
options.rationale: ARM is not feasible with a Bishop of 3 and intact membranes that cannot be reached; the cervix needs further ripening first (RANZCOG; NICE).

options.text: Perform a membrane sweep and reassess in 24 hours
options.rationale: Membrane sweeping is used before formal induction, not after failed prostaglandin induction; it has minimal effect on a Bishop of 3 with a closed cervix (NICE; RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer a third dose of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel now
options.rationale: The local protocol (consistent with most Australian protocols) limits dinoprostone to a maximum of two doses in 24 hours due to tachysystole risk (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Proceed directly to category 4 caesarean section for failed induction
options.rationale: Failed induction is not diagnosed after a single failed prostaglandin course when mechanical methods have not been tried; mechanical ripening with a Foley catheter should be offered first (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert a transcervical Foley catheter (16 Fr) with the balloon inflated to 30-60 mL for 12-24 hours
options.rationale: Mechanical cervical ripening with a Foley catheter is the recommended next step when the cervix remains unfavourable after the maximum dose of prostaglandins, before considering caesarean section. It has comparable efficacy to repeat prostaglandins with a lower risk of tachysystole (RANZCOG; Cochrane 2012).

explanation.summary: When the cervix remains unfavourable (Bishop <=6) after the maximum dose of prostaglandins, the next step is mechanical cervical ripening with a transcervical Foley catheter (or double balloon Cook catheter). This is comparable in efficacy to repeat prostaglandins with less tachysystole and is preferred for women with a previous CS. Caesarean for failed induction is reserved for women who fail mechanical ripening or who are not in active labour after ARM and oxytocin.
key_points: Bishop score: dilatation, length/effacement, station, position, consistency (max 13)
Bishop <=6 = unfavourable cervix; mechanical or pharmacological ripening required
Dinoprostone max 2 doses (Australian protocols)
Foley catheter 16 Fr with 30-60 mL balloon, leave 12-24 h or until expelled
Failed induction diagnosed only after ARM + adequate oxytocin trial without progress
explanation.pearls: Mechanical methods are particularly useful for women with a previous CS because they avoid the increased rupture risk of prostaglandins.

sources.label: KEMH Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour
Cochrane review: Mechanical methods for induction 2012

stem: A 38-year-old G5P4 has had a postpartum haemorrhage following spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg neonate after a labour augmented with oxytocin. She has now received IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 250 mL/h, IM ergometrine 250 microg, IM carboprost 250 microg (three doses), IV tranexamic acid 1 g, and an intrauterine Bakri balloon inflated with 500 mL saline. Total measured blood loss is now 3500 mL and she is being transfused with packed cells, FFP and platelets per the massive transfusion protocol. She is now in theatre and the team is about to commence laparotomy. Her observations are BP 88/52, HR 134, with palpable but cool peripheries. Her fibrinogen on the most recent ROTEM is 1.4 g/L and her platelets are 68. The consultant obstetrician is at the bedside and a gynaecological oncologist is in the hospital on the next case. She has completed her family.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/52 mmHg, HR 134 bpm, T 36.0 degC.
data_table.Examination: Atonic uterus despite Bakri 500 mL. Cool peripheries. In theatre, laparotomy imminent.
data_table.Investigations: Total EBL 3500 mL. Fibrinogen 1.4 g/L. Platelets 68 (x10^9/L). Massive transfusion protocol active.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next surgical step?

options.text: Proceed directly to peripartum hysterectomy without further uterine-sparing attempts
options.rationale: Uterine-sparing surgical options (compression sutures such as B-Lynch, stepwise uterine devascularisation, internal iliac artery ligation) should generally be attempted before peripartum hysterectomy unless there is a specific indication to proceed directly (placenta accreta spectrum, uterine rupture beyond repair, ongoing instability) (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Insert a second intrauterine balloon alongside the first and proceed to interventional radiology
options.rationale: Stacking intrauterine balloons is not a standard technique; if the Bakri has failed and the patient is in theatre, laparotomy with compression suture is the next standard step (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bilateral internal iliac artery ligation as the first surgical step
options.rationale: Internal iliac ligation is a reasonable option but is technically difficult and is usually reserved for after compression suture and uterine artery ligation have failed; it is not the first surgical step in current Australian practice (RANZCOG).

options.text: Re-inflate the Bakri balloon to 750 mL and observe for 30 minutes before any surgical intervention
options.rationale: The Bakri balloon maximum is approximately 500 mL; overfilling risks rupture and does not address ongoing bleeding when the balloon has already failed (manufacturer; RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform a B-Lynch compression suture and stepwise uterine artery ligation before considering hysterectomy
options.rationale: Stepwise surgical escalation in atonic PPH that has failed maximal medical and balloon therapy is: compression suture (B-Lynch) plus uterine artery ligation; if ongoing bleeding, internal iliac ligation or interventional radiology embolisation; then peripartum hysterectomy as a last resort. Uterine-sparing options should be attempted first when the patient is stable enough and the team has the expertise (RANZCOG; KEMH; Royal Women's Vic).

explanation.summary: Surgical escalation for intractable atonic PPH after failed medical and balloon therapy: (1) compression suture such as B-Lynch, (2) stepwise uterine devascularisation (uterine artery ligation, then ovarian artery ligation), (3) internal iliac artery ligation or interventional radiology embolisation, (4) peripartum hysterectomy as last resort. Hysterectomy is performed earlier if the patient is unstable, the uterus is severely damaged, or in placenta accreta spectrum.
key_points: Uterine-sparing options first unless contraindication
B-Lynch suture: vertical compression, leaves uterine cavity accessible
Stepwise uterine devascularisation: uterine arteries then ovarian arteries
Internal iliac (hypogastric) artery ligation: technically demanding
Peripartum hysterectomy: last resort, but do not delay if unstable
Always involve a senior obstetrician early; consider IR if available and patient stable enough
explanation.pearls: The clock matters: do not let pride in uterine-sparing surgery delay hysterectomy in an exsanguinating patient. The right operation is the one that stops the bleeding before the patient dies.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Postpartum haemorrhage clinical guideline
